title: How I built a real-time whale tracker for Polymarket using Node.js and a CLI
summary: Developer built WhaleTrack, a Node.js tool to monitor large cryptocurrency bets (whales) on Polymarket, which has $3.89 billion in World Cup wagers from major wallets. The application fetches live data via CLI and is publicly available at whaletrack.app.
content:
The 2026 World Cup has $3.89 billion bet on it across Polymarket. That's not retail money — that's whales.
I built WhaleTrack to track exactly what those big wallets are doing. Here's the stack:
Backend: Node.js server fetching live data via Bullpen CLI
The site is live at whaletrack.app — would love feedback from devs on the UX and performance.
Happy to open source parts of it if there's interest.

zh.title: 我如何使用Node.js和CLI为Polymarket构建实时鲸鱼追踪器
zh.summary: 开发者构建了WhaleTrack，这是一个Node.js工具，用于监控Polymarket上的大额加密货币赌注（鲸鱼），其中包含来自主要钱包的39亿美元世界杯赌注。该应用通过CLI获取实时数据，可在whaletrack.app公开获取。

title: Privacy Isn't a Checkbox — It's Architecture
summary: This article argues that true privacy requires architectural design decisions rather than policy promises, using Swipe Cleaner as an example where all processing happens locally with zero data leaving the device. Architecture-based privacy is verifiable by users, while policy-based privacy requires ongoing trust in companies.
content:
Privacy isn't a checkbox. It's not a statement in your terms of service. It's not a toggle in your settings app.
Privacy is architecture. And most apps get this backwards.
Here's a simple test for any app that claims to be "privacy-first":
If your server goes down, can anyone still access user data?
If the answer is yes, your privacy is a policy question — not a technical guarantee. You've built a system where trust in the company replaces trust in the code.
Every photo cleaner that uploads your images to a server has already failed this test. The moment data leaves your device, privacy becomes something you promise rather than something you enforce.
When we built Swipe Cleaner, we made one architectural decision that defined everything else: zero data leaves the phone.
This means:
No cloud processing of photos
No API calls that transmit image data
No server-side storage of any kind
Every scan, every classification, every cleanup happens locally
The app is 4.7 MB. That's it. There's nothing to hide because there's nowhere to hide anything.
Companies spend millions on legal teams writing privacy policies that their architecture contradicts. They collect data "to improve the service" while the service could have been designed to not need that data in the first place.



Policy-based privacy
Architecture-based privacy




"We promise not to misuse your data"
"We can't misuse data we never have"


Requires ongoing trust
Verifiable by anyone


Can change with an update to ToS
Requires rewriting the entire app


Compliance-driven
Principle-driven



Here's what I've learned building privacy-focused tools: users can't verify your privacy policy. They can't audit your servers. They can't check if you're actually deleting their data after processing.
But they can verify that an app never sends their photos anywhere. They can check network traffic. They can inspect the binary.
That's the difference between trust and verification. Trust requires faith. Verification requires ev

zh.title: 隐私不是复选框——它是架构
zh.summary: 本文论证真正的隐私需要架构设计决策而非政策承诺，以Swipe Cleaner为例，所有处理都在本地进行，零数据离开设备。基于架构的隐私可由用户验证，而基于政策的隐私需要持续信任公司。

title: Exporting any Bluesky profile's followers with the open API
summary: Bluesky's AT Protocol exposes public profile data through open APIs without authentication, enabling developers to query follower lists without login. The author created an Apify actor for bulk follower exports and competitive audience analysis.
content:
Every big social network locks audience data behind auth walls and anti-bot systems. Bluesky went the other way. The AT Protocol is open by design, so public profile data (bios, follower counts, full follower and following lists) is queryable through a documented API without logging in.
The whole surface is basically two endpoints:
GET https://api.bsky.app/xrpc/app.bsky.actor.getProfile?actor=HANDLE
GET https://api.bsky.app/xrpc/app.bsky.graph.getFollowers?actor=HANDLE&limit=100

There's also getProfiles for batching 25 handles per call. Follower lists paginate with a normal cursor, which still works on the graph endpoints. Search is a different story, cursor pagination 403s there now, but that's a topic for another post.
For one-off lookups, curl is honestly all you need.
Bulk. Thousands of profiles, follower exports that run into six figures, weekly snapshots for tracking. Pagination, rate-limit backoff, and stitching the pages together is boring code that has to run reliably.
I packaged that part as an Apify actor: Bluesky Profile Scraper. Paste handles or profile URLs, optionally turn on follower/following export, and you get JSON or CSV back with a sourceProfile field linking each follower record to the profile it belongs to. $2 per 1,000 records, runs on a schedule if you want snapshots over time.
Vetting an influencer's real audience before paying them. Exporting who follows a competitor and what their bios say. Charting follower growth from weekly runs. And enrichment: find who's talking about you with a mentions monitor, then profile those authors to see their actual reach.
Bluesky is the only major network right now where any of this is straightforward and stable. Worth using while it lasts.

zh.title: 使用开放API导出任何Bluesky资料的关注者
zh.summary: Bluesky的AT协议通过开放API在无需认证的情况下公开资料数据，使开发者能在无需登录的情况下查询关注者列表。作者创建了一个Apify actor用于大批量关注者导出和竞争对手受众分析。

title: Osloq — ให้ AI reproduction เวลาเกิด bug
summary: Osloq is an AI agent that reproduces bugs and identifies root causes by reading GitHub issues, tracing code, testing in sandbox environments, and reporting findings with screenshots and logs. Unlike direct-fix tools, it gives developers information to make their own fix decisions.
content:
Osloq — ใช้ AI หาสาเหตุ bug แทน


เวลา AI coding tools เสนอจะ "fix bug ให้" — เราได้แต่กด Accept หรือไม่ก็ Reject สองปุ่ม สองทางเลือก
แต่เราไม่เคยรู้ว่า:
AI รู้ได้ยังไงว่า bug เกิดจากตรงนี้?
มัน reproduce แล้วหรือแค่อ่านโค้ดแล้วเดา?
ถ้าเรา accept — มันจะพังของอย่างอื่นไหม?
Osloq เลือกทางที่สาม: ไม่ใช่ "fix ให้" — แต่ "หาให้เจอแล้วบอกว่าเกิดอะไรขึ้น"
Osloq เป็น AI agent ที่ทำหน้าที่ "นักสืบ bug"
มีคนเปิด GitHub Issue → Osloq อ่าน → trace โค้ด → reproduce ใน sandbox → ส่งรายงานพร้อมหลักฐาน
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  GitHub Issue: "ปุ่ม submit กดไม่ติดบน Safari"          │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Osloq:                                             │
│  1. อ่าน issue → เข้าใจว่า "ปุ่มไม่ทำงาน"                 │
│  2. trace โค้ด: จาก handler → service → DOM event    │
│  3. reproduce: รัน Safari ใน sandbox → ปุ่มไม่ติดจริง     │
│  4. จับหลักฐาน: logs, screenshots, call stack         │
│  5. สรุป: "event listener ใช้ 'click' แต่ Safari       │
│     บน iOS 18 ไม่ bubble event — ต้องใช้ 'pointerdown' │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Report บน GitHub Issue:                            │
│  📸 screenshot ของ Safari ที่ปุ่มไม่ทำงาน                │
│  📋 console error: "Unhandled Promise Rejection"    │
│  🔗 code path: handler.ts:42 → form.ts:17           │
│  💡 suggestion: เปลี่ยน event type                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

คุณอ่าน report → เข้าใจปัญหา → ตัดสินใจเอง ว่าจะแก้ยังไง




Devin / Sweep AI
Osloq




แนวคิด
"Fix the bug"
"Find the cause"


ทำงานยังไง
เขียนโค้ดใหม่ → เปิด PR
Reproduce → รายงาน evidence


เราเห็นอะไร
PR diff
ภาพ, log, call stack, บทสรุป


ใครตัดสินใจ
AI (เราแค่ merge)

เรา (AI บอกว่าอะไรผิด)


ถ้าผิดพลาด
โค้ดผิดเข้า main
Report ผิด — ไม่กระทบโค้ด


ความเส

zh.title: Osloq — 当发生bug时让AI进行复现
zh.summary: Osloq是一个AI代理，通过阅读GitHub问题、追踪代码、在沙箱环境中测试和报告带有截图和日志的发现来复现bug并识别根本原因。与直接修复工具不同，它为开发人员提供信息以便他们自己做出修复决策。

title: Checking whether ChatGPT actually recommends your product
summary: This article explains how to track whether AI chatbots like ChatGPT and Perplexity recommend your product using official APIs with web search. The author built an Apify actor that automates brand mention tracking and share-of-voice monitoring across multiple engines.
content:
Ask ChatGPT or Perplexity "what's the best note-taking app" and you get a shortlist of three to five names. Either you're on it or you don't exist in that channel. And buying research keeps moving there.
People call measuring this GEO or AEO tracking now. The way most teams do it is pasting questions into chatbots by hand and eyeballing the answers. That stops scaling at about ten questions, and you can't trend it week over week.
Don't scrape the chat UIs. It's fragile, against ToS, and breaks weekly. The engines all have official APIs with web search:
Perplexity's sonar models return answers with citations built in
OpenAI has gpt-4o-search-preview for live web search
Gemini's gemini-2.5-flash supports Google Search grounding
One OpenRouter key covers all three through a single endpoint, which keeps the code boring.
For each buyer question you care about, record four things per engine: was the brand mentioned, how early in the answer, was your domain cited as a source, and how often competitors appeared. That last one gives you share of voice.
I built this as an Apify actor: AI Brand Visibility Tracker. You give it a brand name, domain, competitors, and topics. It generates realistic buyer questions and returns one JSON row per check: brandMentioned, positionScore, brandCited, shareOfVoice, citedDomains, plus a per-engine summary. Schedule it weekly and you have an AI visibility trendline for client reports. $0.05 per check.
citedDomains is the actionable one. It tells you which sites the AI engines treat as sources for your category. Getting mentioned on those specific domains is how you move your visibility. It's link building, except the target list comes from the AI's own citations instead of a guess.

zh.title: 检查ChatGPT是否真的推荐你的产品
zh.summary: 本文解释了如何使用官方API和网络搜索来追踪ChatGPT和Perplexity等AI聊天机器人是否推荐你的产品。作者构建了一个Apify actor，可以跨多个引擎自动化品牌提及跟踪和声量监控。

title: I Opened 3 Security Issues on Microsoft AutoGen and LlamaIndex. Here Is Why
summary: The author discovered three security vulnerabilities in popular AI frameworks (AutoGen and LlamaIndex) including container escapes, agent self-modification, and unbounded recursion using AgentGuard scanner. These findings reveal systemic architectural security gaps in autonomous agent frameworks.
content:
I just opened 3 security issues on two of the most popular AI agent frameworks on GitHub (combined 110K+ stars).
microsoft/autogen#7917: Docker code executor mounts host filesystem into sandboxed containers without trust boundary validation — container escape vector.
microsoft/autogen#7918: Agent self-modification patterns in Canvas memory module — agents can alter their own operating constraints during execution.
run-llama/llama_index#22245: 441 instances of unbounded recursive agent execution across 2,951 files — systemic resource exhaustion risk.
All found with AgentGuard v0.6.2 (pip install dfx-agentguard), an open-source AI agent security scanner.
I have published 12 articles on Dev.to. Average views: 11. GitHub Issues on 50K+ star repos are read by thousands of developers and stay visible for years. This is the correct distribution channel for security findings — direct, unfiltered, and actionable.
The same vulnerability classes appear across all frameworks:
Trust boundary violations (ASI10): agents crossing filesystem and network boundaries
Agent recursion (ASI09): unbounded loops without circuit breakers
Self-modification (ASI10): agents modifying their own state during execution
These are not framework-specific bugs. They are systemic architectural gaps in how we build autonomous agents. Every framework needs guardrails for resource limits, trust boundaries, and behavioral constraints.
AgentGuard detects all of them. 16 rules, 83 tests, 36 benchmark samples, 100 percent detection rate.

zh.title: 我在Microsoft AutoGen和LlamaIndex中提出了3个安全问题，原因如下
zh.summary: 作者在流行的AI框架（AutoGen和LlamaIndex）中发现了三个安全漏洞，包括容器逃逸、代理自修改和使用AgentGuard扫描程序的无界递归。这些发现揭示了自主代理框架中的系统性架构安全缺口。

title: Nobody is monitoring Bluesky, so I built a mentions scraper for it
summary: The author created a Bluesky Mentions Scraper using the AT Protocol's open search endpoint to monitor brand mentions on Bluesky's 27M+ users, filling a gap in social listening tools. The tool handles pagination quirks, sentiment scoring, and author reach analysis at $4 per 1,000 mentions.
content:
I wanted to know when people mention a brand on Bluesky. Simple ask. Turns out Brandwatch, Mention, Hootsuite, basically every social listening tool, still doesn't cover it. They're all busy with X and Instagram while Bluesky sits at 27M+ monthly users.
So I looked at doing it myself and found out something most people miss: you don't need to scrape anything. Bluesky runs on the AT Protocol, which is open by design. Public posts are searchable through a documented endpoint. No login, no API key.
GET https://api.bsky.app/xrpc/app.bsky.feed.searchPosts?q=YOUR_BRAND&sort=latest&limit=100

That returns full post objects. Text, author handle, timestamps, like/repost/reply counts, embedded links, hashtags. Everything you need.
Worth writing down because most tutorials get this wrong now:
The public.api.bsky.app host that older guides point to returns 403 for search. Use api.bsky.app instead.
As of July 2026, unauthenticated search rejects cursor pagination. Page one works fine, page two gets you a 403 with "request forbidden by administrative rules". The nasty part is it looks like rate limiting, but it isn't. The workaround: paginate by time. Use sort=latest, then pass until= with the createdAt of the oldest post from the previous page. Dedupe on uri because the boundary post shows up twice.
I packaged the whole job as an Apify actor: Bluesky Mentions Scraper. Keywords in, clean JSON out. It handles the pagination and retry stuff above, filters replies if you want, scores basic sentiment, and can pull follower counts for each author so you can sort mentions by reach. Runs on a schedule, exports CSV, plugs into Slack or n8n through Apify's integrations. It also works as an MCP tool inside Claude or Cursor.
Pricing is per result, $4 per 1,000 mentions. No subscription.
Brand and product names plus the common misspellings. Competitor names, because share of voice on Bluesky is cheap to win while nobody else is looking. The domain itself, since people share links without nam

zh.title: 没有人在监测Bluesky，所以我建立了一个提及抓取工具
zh.summary: 作者利用AT协议的开放搜索端点创建了Bluesky提及抓取工具，可在Bluesky的2700万+用户中监测品牌提及，填补了社交聆听工具的空白。该工具可处理分页怪癖、情感评分和作者影响力分析，成本为每1000次提及$4。

title: 16 Days of Building in Public — What Actually Moves the Needle
summary: Analysis of indie developer growth over 16 days reveals that Build in Public community posting generates 12x more engagement than raw timeline posting, while dev.to provides lasting organic search traffic. Community placement, consistency, and real product screenshots outweigh polish or influencer engagement.
content:
Disclosure: I work on Opennomos.
After 16 days of consistently posting on X/Twitter about my developer tools project, I have real data on what works and what doesn't for indie dev growth. Here's the unfiltered breakdown.
I built 01MVP — a free set of developer tools (Timestamp Converter and JSON Parser) that handle 90% of my daily workflow. The goal was simple: get developers to use and talk about it.
My daily posting cadence:
1 BIP community post
1 quote tweet to indie devs
1 dev.to article
Bonus: Zhihu (Chinese Q&A)
The clearest signal came from comparing identical content posted in the Build in Public community vs raw timeline posting:



Metric
BIP Community
Raw Posting




Views
36
3


Likes
6
0


Replies
3
0


Reposts
1
0



12x difference. Not "BIP helps a bit" — BIP is the channel, raw posting is basically invisible.
Same topic, same time, same community. The image tweet got engagement immediately. Text-only tweets felt like noise. The images don't need to be polished — a real screenshot of your product beats a designed graphic.
Big accounts (10k+ followers) rarely engage. But indie devs with 100-1000 followers? They reply, they retweet, they check out your profile. The signal-to-noise ratio is way better.
Posting to BIP isn't a "growth hack." It's table stakes. Without a community placement, your tweet competes with the entire algorithm. Inside a community, you're one of maybe 20-50 posts that hour.
Twitter gives you the spike. dev.to gives you the tail. Articles published weeks ago still bring views through search and the dev.to feed. Write once, distribute forever.
Commenting on big influencer posts: 0 interaction so far. Their threads are too noisy.
Posting in Chinese on Zhihu with a new account: Zero traction in 4 days. Cold start on Chinese platforms requires 2-3 weeks of consistent output before the algorithm picks you up.
Waiting for the "perfect" tweet: The 36-view tweet was written in 5 minutes. The overthought ones got 2-8 views.
Daily BIP communi

zh.title: 16天公开构建——什么真正推动了增长
zh.summary: 对16天内独立开发者增长的分析显示，"公开构建"社区发帖产生的参与度是原始时间线发帖的12倍，而dev.to提供持久的有机搜索流量。社区投放、一致性和真实产品截图比打磨或影响力参与更重要。

title: Forecasting from a Government PDF: What I Learned Modeling the Visa Bulletin
summary: The author built tooling to extract and forecast US immigration visa cutoff dates from monthly State Department bulletins, discovering that validation is more critical than modeling due to format inconsistencies. The real engineering challenge was handling evolving document formats and flagging anomalies.
content:
Every month the U.S. State Department publishes the Visa Bulletin. If you have never had to read one, picture a set of grids: rows for green-card categories like EB-2 and EB-3, columns for a handful of countries, and inside each cell either a date or the letter "C" for current. That date is the cutoff. If your priority date is earlier than the cutoff for your category and country, your case can move forward. If it isn't, you wait.
It looks like structured data. It mostly isn't. And the moment you try to turn a year of these bulletins into anything a person can plan around, you run straight into the reason immigration timing feels so unknowable: the source is a monthly snapshot that was never designed to be a time series.
I spent a while building tooling around exactly this problem, and the interesting engineering was almost never where I expected it.
The bulletins are published as web pages and PDFs whose layout drifts over the years. Column headers get reworded. A country gets its own column one year and gets folded into "All Chargeability Areas Except Those Listed" another. Dates appear as 08JAN20, sometimes 01JANUARY2020, sometimes just C, and occasionally U for unavailable, which is a different thing from a date being blank.
None of that is intellectually deep. It is the ordinary tax of pulling a dataset out of documents meant for human eyes. The trap is treating it as a one-time scrape. Because the format shifts, a parser that passed on last year's files can silently mangle this month's, and a silent mangle in a forecast is worse than a crash. So the real work is validation: range-check every parsed date, assert that categories that existed last month still exist, and flag any cell that moved in a direction the rules say is unusual, so a human can look before it feeds anything downstream.
Once you have a clean series of cutoff dates per category and country, the instinct is to fit a line and extrapolate. Resist it. The line does not move the way a naive model a

zh.title: 从政府PDF预测：我在建模签证公告中学到的
zh.summary: 作者构建了工具来从每月的国务院公告中提取和预测美国移民签证截止日期，发现由于格式不一致，验证比建模更为关键。真正的工程挑战是处理不断演变的文档格式并标记异常。

title: What building a contrast checker taught me about WCAG that I had wrong
summary: Building a WCAG contrast checker revealed that compliance requires different thresholds for normal text (4.5:1), large text (3:1), and UI components, and relative luminance weighting differs from intuitive brightness perception. Design intent is preserved by adjusting lightness within color families.
content:
I assumed the WCAG contrast checker for Quietbench would be the simplest tool in the suite — compute a ratio, compare it to a threshold, done. A few things surprised me enough to change how I think about accessibility in my own projects.
"Passing AA" isn't one number. The 4.5:1 ratio everyone quotes only applies to normal text. Large text (18pt+, or 14pt+ bold) only needs 3:1. UI components and graphical objects have their own 3:1 threshold under WCAG 2.1's non-text contrast rule. A tool that only checks one ratio against one threshold is giving wrong answers for half the content on a real page.
AAA isn't just "AA but stricter" in the way I assumed. AAA bumps normal text to 7:1, but the large-text threshold only moves to 4.5:1 — not a flat multiplier. Building the "suggest a fix" feature meant handling these as genuinely different target ratios per content type, not one formula scaled up.
The relative luminance formula is not intuitive. WCAG doesn't compare RGB values directly — it converts to relative luminance first, which weights green far more heavily than red or blue in how humans perceive brightness. Two colors that look similarly "light" or "dark" to the eye can have meaningfully different contrast ratios against the same background, which is exactly why eyeballing contrast doesn't work and a checker is worth having.
Most "suggest a fix" tools I looked at just darken or lighten until they hit the threshold, ignoring the original hue. That technically passes WCAG but can produce a color that no longer matches the brand or design intent. I ended up preserving hue and adjusting lightness within the same color family first, only shifting saturation as a fallback if that alone couldn't reach the target ratio.
None of this is exotic once you know it, but I'd been treating "contrast checking" as basically solved logic before actually building one. It isn't — it's a small pile of specific rules that don't generalize the way intuition suggests.
Live at quietbench.dev/

zh.title: 构建对比度检查器教会我关于WCAG的错误认知
zh.summary: 构建WCAG对比度检查器揭示了合规性需要不同的阈值用于普通文本（4.5:1）、大文本（3:1）和UI组件，相对亮度加权与直观的亮度感知不同。通过在色族内调整亮度来保持设计意图。

title: I Scanned 3 Major AI Agent Frameworks. Here Are the 332 Critical Vulnerabilities
summary: Security scans of three major AI agent frameworks (LlamaIndex, AutoGen, CrewAI) revealed 332 critical vulnerabilities including data exfiltration paths, self-modification vectors, and trust boundary violations, despite 30K+ stars and Fortune 500 deployments. The findings indicate systemic architectural security gaps with known solutions.
content:
I scanned three of the most popular AI agent frameworks with AgentGuard v0.6.1. The results were worse than I expected.



Framework
Files
Findings
CRITICAL
HIGH
MEDIUM




LlamaIndex
2,951
1,003
252

193


AutoGen
549
229

36


CrewAI
84
391
0
0
391



The most popular RAG framework: 252 critical findings. 441 agent loop patterns, 178 data exfiltration paths, 141 trust boundary violations.
Self-modification vectors. Credential exposure in replay logs. MCP host trusts server prompts unsafely. Docker executor mounts host filesystem into sandbox.
Data exfiltration patterns across 391 locations -- agent data flowing to external endpoints without constraints.
Frameworks with 30K+ stars, Fortune 500 production deployments. Findings in the code that ships today. Every finding has a clear fix -- input validation, Pydantic models, sandbox enforcement, log scrubbing. Solved application security problems not yet applied to AI agent code.



GitHub: https://github.com/dockfixlabs/agentguard
Benchmark: 36 samples, 100 percent detection, 0 FP

zh.title: 我扫描了3个主要AI代理框架。以下是332个严重漏洞
zh.summary: 对三个主要AI代理框架（LlamaIndex、AutoGen、CrewAI）的安全扫描发现了332个严重漏洞，包括数据泄露路径、自修改向量和信任边界违规，尽管这些框架拥有30K+的星标和Fortune 500的部署。这些发现表明存在已知解决方案的系统性架构安全漏洞。

A note on the writing: this article, like the book it draws from, was produced in close collaboration with AI - used for research synthesis, structural framing, and editorial development. The operating logic and editorial judgment are the author's.
Ali Sadhik Shaik is a product executive and operator at Astrikos AI, a DBA candidate at Golden Gate University, and the author of The Algorithmic Monographs and The Chief Product and Marketing Officer. Subscribe to The CPMO Playbook for the next chapter.

zh.title: 第9章。第4阶段 - 规模化
zh.summary: 本章概述了产品公司规模化的组织结构和运营节奏，包括团队组成（PM、PMM、增长、品牌、运营）和关键指标如NRR和管道覆盖。这篇文章是通过AI协作进行研究综合和编辑框架制作的。
